summary: (Synthesis unavailable: source id 'f2' is used by more than one merged finding — showing raw agent findings.)

findings.title: `check_not_closed` wrapper is not async-aware, wrapping async methods with a sync wrapper
`Client.close()` decorated with `@check_not_closed` prevents idempotent closing and double-close via `__exit__`
findings.detail:
Decorating `close()` with `@check_not_closed` means that calling `close()` on an already-closed client raises a `RuntimeError`. This is problematic because `__exit__` calls `self.close()` unconditionally. If an exception is raised during the `with` block and the client is somehow closed inside the block (or in cleanup code), `__exit__` will itself raise a `RuntimeError` when trying to close, potentially masking the original exception.

Additionally, the common Python idiom for context managers is that `__exit__` (or `__aexit__`) should be safe to call even if the resource is already closed. The existing test `test_that_client_cannot_be_closed_after_being_closed` actually encodes this as an *expectation*, but this is a correctness hazard when `__exit__` is involved.

A better approach: either don't guard `close()`/`aclose()` with `@check_not_closed` and let them be idempotent, or make `__exit__`/`__aexit__` guard against double-close themselves.

findings.title: `AsyncClient.aclose()` decorated with `@check_not_closed` prevents idempotent closing and breaks `__aexit__`
findings.detail: Same issue as `Client.close()`: decorating `aclose()` with `@check_not_closed` means that `__aexit__` will raise `RuntimeError` if `aclose()` has already been called (e.g. if the user manually called `await client.aclose()` and then the `async with` block exits). This can cause the original exception from the `async with` body to be replaced by or combined with a `RuntimeError`, breaking expected error propagation semantics.

findings.title: Sync Client.close() guarded by check_not_closed but double-close is only tested, not first-close sets _is_closed=True path re-entry
findings.detail: The decorator on `close()` means calling `close()` on an already-closed client raises RuntimeError. This is tested. But there is no test covering the `__exit__` path (context manager exit after manual close): if a user manually calls `client.close()` inside a `with` block, `__exit__` will call `close()` again and raise RuntimeError unexpectedly. This re-entry path is completely uncovered.

findings.title: AsyncClient.__aexit__ calling aclose() after manual aclose() raises RuntimeError – uncovered
findings.detail: Same issue as the sync client: if `aclose()` is called manually inside an `async with` block, `__aexit__` will call `aclose()` again and hit the `check_not_closed` guard, raising RuntimeError. There is no test for this scenario.

findings.title: check_not_closed decorator not tested on async methods other than request/aclose
findings.detail: The decorator is applied to `AsyncClient.request` and `AsyncClient.aclose`. The test `test_that_async_client_cannot_send_request_after_being_closed` only calls `client.get(...)`, which routes through `request()`. None of the convenience methods (post, put, patch, delete, head, options, stream) are tested on a closed AsyncClient, but since the decorator is only on `request` and `aclose` those are covered indirectly. However, there is no test verifying that calling `send()` directly on a closed AsyncClient raises RuntimeError — `send` is not decorated and is only reachable through `request`, so this edge case is latent but worth noting.

findings.title: `@check_not_closed` on `close()` prevents idempotent close, creating a DoS vector in `__exit__`/`__aexit__` error paths
findings.detail: Applying `@check_not_closed` to both `close()` and `aclose()` means that calling `close()` a second time raises a `RuntimeError`. The `__exit__` / `__aexit__` context-manager methods call `close()`/`aclose()` unconditionally. If an exception propagates through the context manager *after* a manual `close()` call inside the block, `__exit__` will swallow the original exception and replace it with a `RuntimeError` raised from the second `close()` call (or, if `__exit__` itself is not guarded, propagate the runtime error instead of the original). This can mask real errors and may be exploitable to suppress security-relevant exceptions (e.g. TLS validation errors). Typically `close()` methods are expected to be idempotent (no-op on second call). Remove the decorator from `close()`/`aclose()`, or make it a no-op when already closed instead of raising.

findings.title: Overly verbose / inconsistent error message
findings.detail: The error message `"{self!r} is closed. We cannot use the closed client!"` is verbose and informal ("We cannot use", trailing exclamation mark). The existing codebase uses terse, impersonal error strings. A message like `"{self!r} is closed"` would be consistent with the surrounding style.

findings.title: Decorator `check_not_closed` does not support async callables
findings.detail: The wrapper defined inside `check_not_closed` is a plain synchronous function. When it wraps an `async def` method (e.g. `AsyncClient.request`, `AsyncClient.aclose`), the decorated method becomes a regular function that returns a coroutine only by accident (the inner `method(self, *args, **kwargs)` call returns a coroutine object that the caller must still `await`). While it happens to work today, it is misleading and inconsistent: the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase for async helpers uses `async def` wrappers. Consider inspecting `asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(method)` and branching, or providing a separate `async_check_not_closed` decorator, to make the intent clear.

findings.title: is_closed property initial state (False) not explicitly tested
findings.detail: Tests only assert `is_closed` is True after closing. There is no test asserting that `client.is_closed` is False for a freshly created client (before any close call), leaving the initial-state branch untested.

findings.title: Error message in RuntimeError may leak internal object repr
findings.detail: The error message in the `check_not_closed` wrapper includes `{self!r}`, which calls `__repr__` on the client object. Depending on what `__repr__` returns (e.g. if it ever includes auth headers, cookies, or other sensitive state in a future version), this could inadvertently expose secrets in log output, error tracking systems (e.g. Sentry), or user-facing error messages. Consider a static message like `'Cannot send a request, as the client has already been closed.'` (similar to what requests/httpcore use) to avoid any future repr leakage.

findings.title: test_that_client_cannot_send_request_after_being_closed uses get() not send() directly
findings.detail: The `send()` method of the sync Client is not decorated with `check_not_closed`, only `request()` is. A test calling `client.send(request)` directly on a closed client should be added to confirm the guard also fires for that path (it currently does, but only indirectly through `request`; if `send` is ever refactored to bypass `request`, coverage would be lost).

findings.title: check_not_closed decorator not tested with proxies set up
findings.detail: The `_transport_for_url` methods and proxy transports are involved when proxies are configured. There is no test that verifies a closed client with proxies raises RuntimeError on request, even though the decorator is on `request` regardless — still, the interaction with `_transport_for_url` after close is untested.

findings.title: Docstring grammar: "Check if the client being closed"
findings.detail: The docstring reads "Check if the client being closed" — it is missing a verb (should be "Check if the client is closed" or, following the style of the other property docstrings in the file, simply "Whether the client is closed").

findings.title: Test function names use a verbose `test_that_` prefix inconsistent with existing tests
findings.detail: All existing tests in this file use the pattern `test_<what_is_tested>` (e.g. `test_get`, `test_post`, `test_pool_limits_deprecated`). The new tests use `test_that_client_…` / `test_that_async_client_…`. Dropping the `that_` (e.g. `test_client_is_closed_after_with_block`) would align with the established naming convention.

findings.title: Test function names use a verbose `test_that_` prefix inconsistent with existing tests
findings.detail: Same as in `test_client.py`: the file's existing tests use concise `test_<behaviour>` names (e.g. `test_get`, `test_post`, `test_stream_response`). New tests should drop the `test_that_` prefix for consistency.

findings.title: Decorator name `check_not_closed` is a verb-phrase that could be more idiomatic
findings.detail: The existing helper utilities in `_utils.py` (e.g. `warn_deprecated`, `get_logger`, `same_origin`) follow a `verb_noun` pattern for functions. `check_not_closed` reads more like an assertion guard; a name such as `require_open` or `enforce_open` would be slightly more idiomatic and clearly convey it raises on violation rather than returning a boolean.
